items.evidence_text:
Client: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement timing, and making sure I’m not messing things up while I’m unemployed and still supporting the kids.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain English and action-oriented. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in these days, even if it’s not wages?
Client: It’s basically investment income. The gross annual income is 92600, mostly interest and dividends.
Advisor: Okay—so interest and dividends, annually. And you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client: Yes, unemployed.

Client: Yes, unemployed.
Advisor: Thanks. On the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in—housing, food, utilities, kids’ stuff, the usual?
Client: Roughly twenty-five hundred a month. Total monthly expenses are 2500.
Advisor: Okay. And do you have any monthly debt payments—credit cards, car loan, student loans—anything like that?
Client: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement timing, and making sure I’m not messing things up while I’m unemployed and still supporting the kids.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain English and action-oriented. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in these days, even if it’s not wages?
Client: It’s basically investment income. The gross annual income is 92600, mostly interest and dividends.
Advisor: Okay—so interest and dividends, annually. And you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client: Yes, unemployed.

Client: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.
Advisor: Great—so no required debt payments. Let’s shift to assets. About how much do you have in investable accounts altogether—brokerage, retirement, anything like that?
Client: Total investable assets are 475600.
Advisor: One sec—just noting that down. Can you walk me through what makes up that 475600, at a high level?
Client: Sure. I’ve got a taxable brokerage account worth 196800.

Client: And another one at an insurance company in private markets—77,500.
Advisor: Understood. And then you mentioned a home as well—do you own your primary residence?
Client: Yes. Primary residence value is 260850, held at my bank.
Advisor: Thanks. Any loans against the house—mortgage, HELOC—or is it free and clear?
Client: No loans outstanding. It’s free and clear.

Client: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement timing, and making sure I’m not messing things up while I’m unemployed and still supporting the kids.

Client: It’s basically investment income. The gross annual income is 92600, mostly interest and dividends.
Advisor: Okay—so interest and dividends, annually. And you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client: Yes, unemployed.

Client: Sure. I’ve got a taxable brokerage account worth 196800.
Advisor: Okay.
Client: Then there’s a retirement account—kind of a 401(k)/IRA bucket—at a brokerage, and that’s 132700.
Advisor: Got it.
Client: I also have an account at an insurance company that’s more like a bank-type holding—cash value-ish—and that’s 68550.

Client: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Now on taxes—last time you mentioned you tend to land in the thirty-two-ish bracket. What bracket should I use as our baseline?
Client: Use 32%.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And just to connect the dots—your income is still that investment income, the interest and dividends, at 92600 annually, right?
Client: Yes—92600 a year from interest and dividends. I’m unemployed otherwise.

Client: Yeah, three kids. That’s the big driver for me.
Advisor: Makes sense. Next one: what state are you a resident of for tax purposes?
Client: TX.
Advisor: Texas—got it. And country—are we planning everything as US-based?
Client: Yes, US.

Advisor: Okay—so interest and dividends, annually. And you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client: Yes, unemployed.
Advisor: Thanks. On the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in—housing, food, utilities, kids’ stuff, the usual?
Client: Roughly twenty-five hundred a month. Total monthly expenses are 2500.
Advisor: Okay. And do you have any monthly debt payments—credit cards, car loan, student loans—anything like that?

Client: Total investable assets are 475600.
Advisor: One sec—just noting that down. Can you walk me through what makes up that 475600, at a high level?
Client: Sure. I’ve got a taxable brokerage account worth 196800.
Advisor: Okay.
Client: Then there’s a retirement account—kind of a 401(k)/IRA bucket—at a brokerage, and that’s 132700.

Advisor: Got it. And marital status—are you currently married, partnered, single, divorced… what should I put down?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Okay. Now, when we talk about how we’ll work together—are you looking for this to be through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?
Client: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. And then risk—when we build the investment mix, do you see yourself as conservative, moderate, or more growth-oriented?

Advisor: Okay. Now, when we talk about how we’ll work together—are you looking for this to be through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?
Client: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. And then risk—when we build the investment mix, do you see yourself as conservative, moderate, or more growth-oriented?
Client: Growth. I’m growth, but I don’t want anything that puts the kids’ stability at risk.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Growth doesn’t have to mean reckless—just means we’re aiming for more long-term upside with a plan for the bumps.

Advisor: First one’s simple: in your household, is it just you as the adult, or is there another adult in the household?
Client: It’s just me—1 adult.
Advisor: Got it. And marital status—are you currently married, partnered, single, divorced… what should I put down?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Okay. Now, when we talk about how we’ll work together—are you looking for this to be through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?

Client: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement timing, and making sure I’m not messing things up while I’m unemployed and still supporting the kids.

Advisor: Okay.
Client: Then there’s a retirement account—kind of a 401(k)/IRA bucket—at a brokerage, and that’s 132700.

Advisor: Perfect. Next time we’ll translate “growth” into a couple portfolio options and tie it back to retirement at 64 and supporting the three kids.
Advisor: Joseph, before we hop off—one last personal-detail bucket that helps with planning. When you think of your career background, what kind of work have you done most of your life?
Client: I’m a professional—most of my career has been in professional roles.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm where things stand today—are you currently working, between roles, retired… how would you describe your employment status right now?
Client: Unemployed.

Advisor: Hi Joseph—good to meet you. I’m glad you made the time. Before we dig into numbers, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement timing, and making sure I’m not messing things up while I’m unemployed and still supporting the kids.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain English and action-oriented. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in these days, even if it’s not wages?
Client: It’s basically investment income. The gross annual income is 92600, mostly interest and dividends.

Advisor: Okay, 68,550 in that insurance-held cash-type account.
Client: And another one at an insurance company in private markets—77,500.
Advisor: Understood. And then you mentioned a home as well—do you own your primary residence?
Client: Yes. Primary residence value is 260850, held at my bank.
Advisor: Thanks. Any loans against the house—mortgage, HELOC—or is it free and clear?

Client: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement timing, and making sure I’m not messing things up while I’m unemployed and still supporting the kids.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it plain English and action-oriented. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in these days, even if it’s not wages?
Client: It’s basically investment income. The gross annual income is 92600, mostly interest and dividends.
Advisor: Okay—so interest and dividends, annually. And you’re currently unemployed, right?
Client: Yes, unemployed.

Advisor: Okay. And even though you’re unemployed from a job standpoint, you still have that annual income coming in from investments—what gross annual income number should I anchor to?
Client: Use 92600 as my gross annual income.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. One admin thing so I don’t mix up notes later: you’re the only person on the plan, and you’re Client 1, correct?
Client: Correct, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Great. And just so I’m not missing anything—there isn’t any other earned income besides that interest and dividends, right?
